elements.sent-2411-001: Infrared and visible (IR-VIS) image fusion has gained significant attention for its broad application value.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, existing methods often neglect the complementary role of infrared image in restoring visible image features under hazy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this, we propose a joint learning framework that utilizes infrared image for the restoration and fusion of hazy IR-VIS images.
elements.sent-2411-004: To mitigate the adverse effects of feature diversity between IR-VIS images, we introduce a prompt generation mechanism that regulates modality-specific feature incompatibility.
elements.sent-2411-005: This creates a prompt selection matrix from non-shared image information, followed by prompt embeddings generated from a prompt pool.
elements.sent-2411-006: These embeddings help generate candidate features for dehazing.
elements.sent-2411-007: We further design an infrared-assisted feature restoration mechanism that selects candidate features based on haze density, enabling simultaneous restoration and fusion within a single-stage framework.
elements.sent-2411-008: To enhance fusion quality, we construct a multi-stage prompt embedding fusion module that leverages feature supplementation from the prompt generation module.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our method effectively fuses IR-VIS images while removing haze, yielding clear, haze-free fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-010: In contrast to two-stage methods that dehaze and then fuse, our approach enables collaborative training in a single-stage framework, making the model relatively lightweight and suitable for practical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-011: Experimental results validate its effectiveness and demonstrate advantages over existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: Infrared and visible (IR-VIS) image fusion effectively combines the unique information from both infrared and visible images, creating a composite image that integrates their complementary features.
elements.sent-2411-013: This fused image not only provides a comprehensive and accurate scene representation but also significantly aids observers in understanding and analyzing complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consequently, this technology holds tremendous potential and value in fields such as military reconnaissance, aerospace, environmental monitoring, and medical diagnostics.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[height=2.4in,width=3.0in]{1.png} \caption{Comparison of existing method and our method for hazy IR-VIS image fusion. (a) The existing method, (b) Our method.} \label{Fig1}
elements.sent-2411-015: In recent years, significant progress has been made in IR-VIS image fusion; however, existing methods generally assume that the input visible images are of good visual quality.
elements.sent-2411-016: In hazy conditions, visible images are affected by haze, resulting in unclear imagery, which makes it difficult for these methods to generate clear, haze-free fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-017: Traditional approaches typically address this issue by first applying a dehazing algorithm to the hazy image and then fusing the dehazed image with the infrared image, as shown in Figure \ref{Fig1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although this two-stage strategy is feasible, it fails to integrate the dehazing and fusion tasks into a unified framework for joint training, making it challenging to balance the relationship between the two tasks.
elements.sent-2411-019: While dehazed images may show good dehazing performance, they are not always optimal for subsequent fusion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, the two-stage process of dehazing followed by fusion involves different methodologies, reducing the model's compactness.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address the issues arising from the two-stage processing paradigm, Li et al. \cite{30 proposed the all-weather multi-modality image fusion method, which achieves image restoration and fusion under various complex weather conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, this method fails to effectively coordinate the differences between the different restoration tasks, limiting further improvements in restoration and fusion performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: In response, Yi et al. \cite{31 introduced a method called Text-IF, which guides the fusion of degraded images using semantic text.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Nevertheless, this approach relies on pre-input text descriptions, increasing the complexity of model deployment.
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, while Text-IF is designed for the restoration and fusion of multiple types of degraded images, it faces challenges in balancing fusion performance across various degradation scenarios without compromising individual task performance.
elements.sent-2411-026: In response to the challenge of IR-VIS image fusion and restoration under hazy conditions, we propose an infrared-assisted joint learning framework, as shown in Figure \ref{Fig1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-027: To mitigate the impact of discrepancies between IR-VIS images on hazy image feature restoration, we design a prompt generation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-028: It leverages non-shared information from input images to create a prompt selection matrix that selects and generates prompt embeddings from a prompt pool.
elements.sent-2411-029: These embeddings act as candidate features to aid in the recovery of hazy image features.
elements.sent-2411-030: For effective restoration of haze-affected features, we construct an infrared-assisted feature restoration module.
elements.sent-2411-031: It guides the selection of candidate features based on haze density to restore visible image features impacted by haze, enabling the joint processing of restoration and fusion within a single-stage framework.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this process, our focus shifts from solely enhancing the restoration of hazy visible images to emphasizing how restored features can further improve the quality of the fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-033: To further enhance the fusion effect, we propose a multi-stage prompt embedding fusion module, which strengthens feature restoration and fusion with the help of the feature supplementation capability of the prompt generation.
elements.sent-2411-034: The proposed method not only effectively fuses IR-VIS images but also eliminates the interference of haze, producing clear and haze-free fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-035: Compared to the traditional two-stage approach of first dehazing and then fusing, our method more fully exploits the correlation between dehazing and fusion tasks, achieving a balance between them through a single-stage framework with collaborative training.
elements.sent-2411-036: Furthermore, the model structure is relatively lightweight and compact, facilitating practical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-037: Unlike existing multi-task fusion frameworks, our method is specifically designed for IR-VIS image fusion and restoration under hazy conditions, demonstrating excellent fusion and restoration performance.
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, our approach enriches the technical system for IR-VIS image fusion under hazy conditions and provides a new perspective for the restoration and fusion of low-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-039: In summary, the main contributions and advantages of our method are reflected in the following aspects:.
elements.sent-2411-041: An infrared-assisted joint learning framework for IR-VIS image fusion in hazy conditions is proposed, enabling collaborative training of dehazing and fusion tasks in a single stage.
elements.sent-2411-042: Compared to traditional two-stage methods, this framework more efficiently leverages the correlation between dehazing and fusion tasks, allowing them to complement each other and achieve a balanced outcome.
elements.sent-2411-043: Prompt Generation Module and Multi-Stage Fusion.
elements.sent-2411-044: A prompt generation module is designed to create a prompt selection matrix leveraging non-shared information to intelligently select and embed prompts that assist visible image restoration under hazy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-045: This approach not only enhances the restoration of visible image features but also minimizes the adverse impact of modality differences between IR-VIS features.
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, a multi-stage feature fusion mechanism based on prompt embeddings is proposed to facilitate feature compensation and refinement, further improving the fusion result.
elements.sent-2411-048: The proposed method adopts a single-stage processing framework, making it more compact than the traditional two-stage dehazing and fusion approach, thereby facilitating a better balance between the two tasks.
elements.sent-2411-049: Furthermore, the method efficiently coordinates the dehazing and fusion, enabling the restored image features to more effectively support the generation of clear, haze-free, high-quality fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-050: This design not only enhances the fusion effect but also improves the model's applicability.
elements.sent-2411-051: In IR-VIS image fusion, traditional methods based on multi-scale transforms and sparse representations \cite{14,15,13,17,1 remain relevant.}
elements.sent-2411-052: However, deep learning-based techniques have become mainstream.
elements.sent-2411-053: These methods can be categorized into three types: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based methods \cite{2,4,5 , hybrid CNN-Transformer methods \cite{3,20,21,22 , and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based methods \cite{12,18,19,7,16,11,10 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: CNN-based methods extract features from input images and perform fusion using specialized modules, enhancing image details and contrast.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, CNNs are limited in modeling long-range dependencies, which impacts fusion quality in complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-056: In contrast, Transformers excel at capturing long-range dependencies but struggle with local details and edges.
elements.sent-2411-057: Hybrid methods, such as AFT \cite{20 , YDTR \cite{21 , and HitFusion \cite{22 , combine CNN with Transformer to model local and global information, improving fusion performance.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[height=2.4in,width=6.4in]{2.png} \caption{Overall framework of the proposed method. The input IR and hazy VIS image pair $\left\{{{\bm{I}}_{ir}}, {{\bm{I}}_{vi}} \right\}$ is processed by the PGM to obtain features \(\left\{ {{\bm{F}}_{ir}},{{\bm{F}}_{vi}} \right\}\) and a prompt \({{\bm{\hat P}}_{ir}}\) for \({{\bm{F}}_{ir}}\). Through the PEB, the prompt embedding \({{\bm{\hat P}}_{ir}}\) is used to refine the IR feature ${{\bm{F}}_{ir}}$, reducing redundant information and generating the refined IR feature ${{\hat{\bm{F}}_{ir}}}$. The HDE module \cite{34} estimates the haze density in the VIS features to dynamically adjust the proportion of injected IR information, preventing excessive IR injection. The Transformer block removes degradation from the input features to obtain haze-free features. In the MsPE-FM, the haze-free VIS features and IR features are combined and passed to the Fusion Block for feature fusion. The PGM and PEB further are used to enhance the IR-VIS complementary information, reconstructing the final fused image.} \label{Fig2}
elements.sent-2411-058: In GAN-based methods, FusionGAN \cite{12 uses a single discriminator to fuse IR-VIS images, which does not maintain modality balance, leading to biased fusion results.}
elements.sent-2411-059: To address this, subsequent research introduced dual discriminator-based GAN methods.
elements.sent-2411-060: For example, LGMGAN \cite{18 combines a Conditional GAN with dual discriminators to fuse multi-modality information effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-061: DDcGAN \cite{19 uses dual discriminators for multi-resolution fusion, improving consistency across scales.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Moreover, AttentionFGAN \cite{11 integrates an attention mechanism to focus on important feature regions, significantly enhancing fusion performance.}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, these methods assume that the images to be fused are of high quality, which makes it challenging to produce high-quality fusion results under hazy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-064: Under complex imaging conditions, various factors affect the quality of visible images.
elements.sent-2411-065: Thus, achieving high-quality fusion results under these conditions has become a crucial research direction in the field of IR-VIS image fusion.
elements.sent-2411-066: In low-light conditions, PIAFusion \cite{36 improves IR-VIS image fusion by introducing an illumination-aware loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-067: DIVFusion \cite{24 enhances dark areas, details, and reduces color distortion by separating scene illumination and enhancing texture contrast, achieving high-quality fusion in nighttime conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-068: IAIFNet \cite{25 uses an illumination enhancement network along with adaptive difference fusion and salient object awareness modules to better fuse features in IR-VIS images.}
elements.sent-2411-069: LENFusion \cite{26 generates high-contrast fusion results through three stages: brightness adjustment, enhancement, and feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-070: For low-resolution images, HKD-FS \cite{27 employs knowledge distillation to convert low-resolution IR-VIS images into high-resolution outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-071: MLFusion \cite{28 incorporates meta-learning into the IR-VIS image fusion framework, enabling fusion from inputs of any resolution to outputs of any resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-072: To address the degradation of visible images under complex conditions, a decomposition-based, interference-aware fusion method was proposed \cite{29 , which effectively fuses degraded IR-VIS images, including noisy, overexposed, and snowy images, but does not address hazy conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-073: To address this, AWFusion \cite{30 introduces a clear feature prediction module based on the atmospheric scattering model, enabling dehazing capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, AWFusion also considers other weather conditions like snow and rain, which reduces its effectiveness in hazy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-075: To balance these tasks, Text-IF \cite{31 uses text guidance for fusion by generating modulation parameters to control cross-attention outputs, but it is not specifically designed for hazy IR-VIS fusion, resulting in suboptimal performance.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Additionally, the need for text input limits its convenience.
elements.sent-2411-077: VIFNet \cite{32 restores hazy images using infrared images, but it focuses only on dehazing and does not address fusion.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In contrast, this paper specifically focuses on IR-VIS fusion in hazy conditions to achieve clear, haze-free results.
elements.sent-2411-079: As shown in Figure \ref{Fig2 , our method comprises three core modules: the Infrared-Assisted Feature Restoration Module (IA-FRM), the Prompt Generation Module (PGM), and the Multi-stage Prompt Embedding Fusion Module (MsPE-FM).}
elements.sent-2411-080: IA-FRM leverages infrared image features to assist in restoring lost information in heavily hazy regions of visible images, making it easier to restore these hazy areas.
elements.sent-2411-081: PGM generates a set of prompts to overcome the limitations of infrared images when assisting in the restoration of features in these dense hazy regions.
elements.sent-2411-082: Using the restored visible image features and prompts from PGM, MsPE-FM performs the fusion of IR-VIS image features, reconstructing a haze-free fused result.
elements.sent-2411-083: Infrared imaging sensors maintain performance in hazy conditions, allowing them to penetrate heavy haze.
elements.sent-2411-084: In a rigorously registered pair of IR-VIS images $\bm{I _{ir , \bm{I _{vi}$ provided to the model, we assume that only the visible image $\bm{I _{vi}$ contains haze, while the infrared image $\bm{I _{ir}$ is unaffected by haze.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The core challenge of this work is to effectively utilize the infrared image $\bm{I _{ir}$ to restore the visible image $\bm{I _{vi}$ and then fuse them.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: However, the significant modality differences between IR-VIS images make it difficult to rely solely on the infrared image $\bm{I _{ir}$ to recover the details lost in the hazy visible image.}
elements.sent-2411-087: To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes the PGM, which generates a prompt embedding to create compensatory features that address the limitations of infrared features.
elements.sent-2411-088: As shown in Figure \ref{Fig2 , in the PGM, we first utilize an encoder constructed with Restormer \cite{33 to perform feature encoding on the input registered IR-VIS images $\{{\bm{I _{ir , {\bm{I _{vi}\}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: As depicted in Figure \ref{Fig2 (a), Restormer consists of a self-attention layer and a feed-forward network layer.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The features output by the Restormer encoder are denoted as ${{\bm{F _{vi \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H \times W}$ and ${{\bm{F _{ir \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H \times W}$, where $C$, $H$, and $W$ represent the number of channels, height, and width of the features, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Additionally, in this module, the features of the hazy visible image ${\bm{I _{vi}$ and the infrared image ${\bm{I _{ir}$ are processed by: \begin{equation}\bm{F}_{vi-ir} = \bm{F}_{vi} - \bm{F}_{ir}\end{equation} to remove the shared information and highlight the unique information.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The resulting difference $\bm{F _{vi-ir}$ is then fed into a weight prediction network composed of Convolutional (Conv) layer, GAP, Linear layer, and Softmax, resulting in a weight matrix ${{\bm{W _p \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H \times W}$ for selecting prompt information from the prompt pool $\bm{P _{ir}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: At this stage, the prompt embedding generated for compensating the infrared image features can be represented as: \begin{equation}{{\bm{\hat{P}}}_{ir}} = {\rm{Conv}}_{3 \times 3} \left( {\rm{Conv}}_{1 \times 1} \left( {{\bm{W}}_p} \odot {{\bm{P}}_{ir}} \right) \right)\end{equation} where ${\bm{P _{ir \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times H \times W}$ is a learnable prompt pool, ${\rm{Conv _{3 \times 3}$ and ${\rm{Conv _{1 \times 1}$ denote $3\times3$ and $1\times1$ Conv layers, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The resulting ${\bm{\hat{P _{ir }$ is then fed, along with the infrared image features ${\bm{F _{ir }$, into the Prompt Embedding Block (PEB) to obtain the features ${\bm{\hat{F _{ir }$ for compensating the dense haze regions in the visible image ${\bm{I _{vi}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Feature restoration assisted by infrared image
elements.sent-2411-095: To effectively utilize the information provided by the infrared image $\bm{I _{ir}$ for restoring features in haze regions, we design the IA-FRM.}
elements.sent-2411-096: As shown in Figure \ref{Fig2 , when restoring features in hazy images, regions with higher haze density should receive more focus.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, it is essential to estimate the haze density in the input images.
elements.sent-2411-098: To achieve this, we adopt the method from \cite{34 to estimate the haze density in the input image features, as illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig2 (c).}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, the dark channel information of ${{\bm{F _{vi }$ is obtained through the Dark Channel Generation (DCG) operation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The resulting dark channel information, along with the feature ${\tilde{\bm{F _{vi}$, is then fed into the Atmospheric Light Estimation (ALE) module to predict the atmospheric light, denoted as $\bm{A}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Here, ${\tilde{\bm{F _{vi}$ is obtained by applying Channel Average Pooling (CAP) to ${{\bm{F _{vi }$, followed by channel replication.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Based on $\bm{A}$ and ${\tilde{\bm{F _{vi}$, we can obtain the initial transmission map for each pixel location through the Get Haze Density (GHD) block, as shown in Eq. (3): \begin{equation}{\bm{T}} = \mathbf{1} - \omega \cdot \text{DCG}\left( \tilde {\bm{F}}_{vi} \odot \bm{A}^{-1} \right)\end{equation} where $\omega$ is a constant that adjusts the effect of the DCG prediction, set to 0.95 as suggested in \cite{34 ; $\mathbf{1 \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \times H \times W}$ is a matrix of ones; and ${{\bm{A ^{-1 }$ represents the element-wise reciprocal of the atmospheric light $\bm{A}$, which is broadcast along the channel dimension to match the dimension of ${{\bm{\tilde {F _{vi }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Then, in the GHD block, the transmission map $\bm{T}$ is refined using a guided filter, yielding the refined transmission map $\bm{T '$.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Since the transmission map is inversely proportional to the haze density, we estimate the haze density $\bm{H}$ through the following equation: \begin{equation}{\bm{H}} = \mathbf{1} - {\bm{T}}'\end{equation} In $\bm{H}$, the higher the haze density, the larger the corresponding value.
elements.sent-2411-105: Therefore, $\bm{H}$ is used to select the information for restoring $\bm{F _{vi}$ from $\bm{\hat {F _{ir}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: In this process, we use $\left( {\mathbf{1 - {\bm{H \right)$ to remove the information from regions with heavier haze in ${\bm{F _{vi}$ and replace it with corresponding information from $\bm{\hat {F _{ir}$ to assist in restoring these regions.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The specific process can be formulated as: \begin{equation}{\bm{\hat {F}}}_{vi} = TF\left({\bm{\hat {F}}}_{ir} \odot {\bm{H}} + {\bm{F}}_{vi} \odot \left( \mathbf{1} - {\bm{H}} \right) + {\bm{F}}_{vi}\right)\end{equation} where $TF$ denotes Transformer block.
elements.sent-2411-108: To ensure the quality of ${\bm{\hat {F _{vi}$, it is passed through a $3\times3$ Conv to obtain the dehazed image ${\bm{\hat {I _{vi}$, and $L_1$-loss is used to optimize the network: \begin{equation}\ell_1 = \left\| {\bm{\hat I}}_{vi} - {\bm{I}}_{vi,gt} \right\|_1\end{equation} Here, ${\bm{I _{vi,gt}$ represents the corresponding ground truth haze-free visible image.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: With the assistance of infrared features ${\bm{\hat{F _{ir}$, we obtain the dehazed visible image features ${\bm{\hat{F _{vi}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: These features are then fused with the infrared image features ${\bm{F _{ir}$ to reconstruct a dehazed fusion result.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: This approach enables us to fuse hazy IR-VIS images within a single framework, producing a dehazed fusion output.
elements.sent-2411-112: In this process, an effective fusion method is essential to achieve high-quality fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-113: To prevent residual haze in ${\bm{\hat{F _{vi}$ from affecting the fusion, we propose the MsPE-FM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: As shown in Figure~\ref{Fig2 , in the MsPE-FM, the restored feature $\bm{\hat{F _{vi}$ and the initial feature $\bm{F _{ir}$ are input into the PGM to obtain the prompt embedding $\bm{\hat P _{vi ^1$ at the first stage of the fusion process.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: After concatenating $\bm{\hat{F _{vi}$ and $\bm{F _{ir}$, the result is passed through the Restormer \cite{33 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: This output, along with $\bm{\hat P _{vi ^1$, is then input into the PEB to obtain the fusion result for the next stage.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: In the second stage, $\bm{\hat P _{vi ^1$ is first passed through a $1 \times 1$ Conv layer to adjust the number of channels, resulting in the adjusted prompt embedding $\bm{\hat P _{vi ^2$, which adapts to the changes in feature channels during the second-stage feature extraction.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: In this fusion process, five fusion blocks, each consisting of prompt embeddings, a Restormer, and a PEB, are used to achieve the fusion of IR-VIS features.
elements.sent-2411-119: Within these fusion blocks, two residual connections are employed to prevent information loss.
elements.sent-2411-120: Finally, the fused features pass through a $1 \times 1$ Conv layer to reconstruct the fused result $\bm{I _f$.}

elements.sent-2411-122: Additionally, to ensure that the fused image maintains consistent pixel intensity with both the IR and VIS images, we utilize a pixel intensity consistency loss function $\ell_{int}$ to update the network parameters: \begin{equation}{\ell_{int}} = \frac{1}{{HW}} \sum_{i=1}^3 \left\| {\bm{I}}_{f}^{i} - \max \left( {\bm{I}}_{ir}, {\bm{I}}_{vi,gt}^{i}\right) \right\|_1\end{equation} The total loss is then formulated as: \begin{equation}{\ell_{total}} = {\ell_{int}} + {\ell_\nabla} + \alpha {\ell_1}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter that adjusts the contribution of the $L_1$-loss in this optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-124: In this work, we utilize 1,083 IR-VIS image pairs from the MSRS dataset \cite{36 as the training set.}
elements.sent-2411-125: For the test set, we select 361 pairs from MSRS (ensuring no overlap with the training set), 50 pairs from RoadScene \cite{38 , and 100 pairs from M$^3$FD \cite{16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-126: To generate hazy image pairs, we apply the atmospheric scattering model \cite{middleton1952vision to introduce haze into the visible images in both the training and test sets.}
elements.sent-2411-127: All experiments are conducted using the PyTorch framework on a single 24GB NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-128: During training, images are randomly cropped to $256 \times 256$ patches, with data augmentation techniques such as horizontal and vertical flipping applied.
elements.sent-2411-129: The model is trained for a total of 300 epochs, using a batch size of 6 and the AdamW optimizer \cite{39 .}
elements.sent-2411-130: The initial learning rate is set to $2 \times 10^{-4}$ and is gradually reduced to $2 \times 10^{-6}$ following a cosine annealing schedule.

elements.sent-2411-131: To objectively evaluate the fusion performance of different methods, we adopt five commonly used image quality assessment metrics: Mutual Information ($Q_{MI}$) \cite{40 , Gradient-based Fusion Performance ($Q_{AB/F}$) \cite{41 , Chen-Varshney Metric ($Q_{CV}$) \cite{42 , Sum of Correlation of Differences ($Q_{SCD}$) \cite{43 , and Visual Information Fidelity ($Q_{VIF}$) \cite{44 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: These metrics assess the quality of the fusion results, using clear source images (without haze) as reference images when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-133: Additionally, to evaluate the perceptual quality of the dehazing effects within the fusion results, we employ the Perceptual Index ($Q_{PI}$) \cite{45 , Natural Image Quality Evaluator ($Q_{NIQE}$) \cite{46 , and Spatial Frequency ($Q_{SF}$) \cite{47 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: According to the evaluation criteria, lower $Q_{CV}$, $Q_{PI}$, and $Q_{NIQE}$ values indicate better fusion performance, while higher values for the remaining metrics signify improved quality.
elements.sent-2411-135: There are two existing methodologies for fusing hazy visible images with infrared images.
elements.sent-2411-136: The first methodology involves initially applying advanced image dehazing algorithms to remove haze from the visible images, followed by fusing the dehazed images with the infrared images.
elements.sent-2411-137: For this purpose, we select the latest and most effective dehazing methods, specifically DIACMP \cite{48 and Dehazeformer \cite{49 .}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Next, we apply representative IR-VIS image fusion methods, such as MLFusion \cite{28 , U2Fusion \cite{38 , LRRNet \cite{5 , ALFusion \cite{3 , TIMFusion \cite{50 , and MRFS \cite{51 , to fuse the dehazed visible images with the infrared images.}}}}}}

elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{2.png} \caption{Overall framework of the proposed method. The input IR and hazy VIS image pair $\left\{{{\bm{I}}_{ir}}, {{\bm{I}}_{vi}} \right\}$ is processed by the PGM to obtain features \(\left\{ {{\bm{F}}_{ir}},{{\bm{F}}_{vi}} \right\}\) and a prompt \({{\bm{\hat P}}_{ir}}\) for \({{\bm{F}}_{ir}}\). Through the PEB, the prompt embedding \({{\bm{\hat P}}_{ir}}\) is used to refine the IR feature ${{\bm{F}}_{ir}}$, reducing redundant information and generating the refined IR feature ${{\hat{\bm{F}}_{ir}}}$. The haze density esitimation (HDE) module \cite{34} estimates the haze density in the VIS features to dynamically adjust the proportion of injected IR information, preventing excessive IR injection. The Transformer block removes degradation from the input features to obtain haze-free features. In the MsPE-FM, the haze-free VIS features and IR features are combined and passed to the Fusion Block for feature fusion. The PGM and PEB further are used to enhance the IR-VIS complementary information, reconstructing the final fused image.} \label{Fig2}

elements.sent-2411-286: A Our method involves two key hyperparameters: $\alpha$, used to balance the $L1$-loss, and $M$, representing the number of Fusion Blocks in MsPE-FM.
elements.sent-2411-287: During training, they are set to 2 and 5, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-288: To validate the rationale behind these choices, we conduct a detailed analysis of their impact on the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-289: The impact of $\alpha$ on model performance.

elements.sent-2411-292: The results indicate that when $\alpha$ is too small, the model performance does not reach its optimal level.
elements.sent-2411-293: Similarly, when $\alpha$ is too large, performance declines as well.
elements.sent-2411-294: The model achieves the best overall performance when $\alpha = 2$, which validates the choice of $\alpha = 2$ in this study.
elements.sent-2411-295: The impact of $M$ on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-296: To analyze the impact of different values of $M$ on the model performance, we fix $\alpha$ at 2 and adjust $M$ within the range of [1, 7] to observe how the model performance changes.
elements.sent-2411-297: As shown in Table \ref{tab9 , the overall performance of the model improves with the number of Fusion Blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-298: However, from the results for $M=5$ and $M=7$, it is evident that the performance gain has plateaued.
elements.sent-2411-299: Considering that increasing the number of Fusion Blocks significantly raises the model's parameter count, we ultimately set $M$ to 5.

elements.sent-2411-303: Although our method effectively handles hazy image fusion with high quality, real-world scenarios may still present challenging weather conditions such as low light, snow, and rain.
elements.sent-2411-304: Our approach does not currently account for the impact of these factors.
elements.sent-2411-305: While some existing methods have attempted to address this issue, they fail to effectively balance the impact of different degradations on the fusion results.
elements.sent-2411-306: Furthermore, these methods typically assume that the degradation types in the images have been encountered by the model, and the model performance tends to be suboptimal in scenarios with unseen degradations.
elements.sent-2411-307: Therefore, future work will focus on designing a multi-degradation joint processing framework to enable the model to effectively perform fusion and restoration even in scenarios with unseen degradations.
elements.sent-2411-308: The lightweight and compact model structure also ensures practical deployment, making it a valuable solution for hazy image restoration and fusion.
